title: T-Mobile moving tens of thousands of virtual machines off VMware amid lawsuit
gist: T-Mobile is suing Broadcom over refused VMware perpetual-license support renewals while migrating tens of thousands of VMs off VMware, one of several large enterprise disputes (AT&T, Tesco) triggered by Broadcom's shift to expensive subscription bundles.

insights.claim: After acquiring VMware, Broadcom stopped selling perpetual licenses in favor of subscriptions and bundled products into fewer, more expensive bundles.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Broadcom is monetizing its VMware base via forced subscription conversion; expect elevated churn and litigation from large customers.
insights.locator: The mobile company claimed that in 2023

insights.claim: Broadcom refused T-Mobile's attempt to extend perpetual-license support for a third year at $5,288,398.45, citing end of availability of all perpetual products.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Broadcom is willing to forgo multi-million-dollar support renewals to force subscription adoption, revealing its pricing strategy priorities.
insights.quote: Broadcom announced end of available of all perpetual products, which includes Stated Out Year Renewals for perpetual support
insights.locator: When T-Mobile tried to extend support

insights.claim: T-Mobile at one point offered $20 million for two years of VMware software updates and support, citing security risks to its network and business.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Shows the steep switching and continuity costs locked-in VMware customers face, underpinning Broadcom's pricing power.
insights.locator: At one point, T-Mobile wanted support so badly

themes.theme: Scale of migration and switching costs

insights.claim: T-Mobile runs tens of thousands of virtual machines on VMware across approximately 303,140 CPU cores and over 1,000 applications, making migration technically arduous and time-consuming.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Quantifies VMware lock-in at a major carrier; migration difficulty is the moat Broadcom is monetizing.
insights.locator: In its complaint, T-Mobile said it has tens of thousands

insights.claim: A judge granted T-Mobile an injunction for VMware support from October 2025 through August 3, 2026, for $5.28 million plus a $500,000 undertaking.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Courts are willing to compel interim support, giving large customers a temporary bridge while migrating off VMware.
insights.locator: A judge granted T-Mobile an injunction

insights.claim: Broadcom claimed it incurred $24 million providing T-Mobile support for six VMware products and three dedicated account managers, but T-Mobile said it doesn't use three of the six products and opened only two service cases this year.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Dispute over actual support usage highlights the gap between bundled pricing and value delivered.
insights.locator: In a filing last month, Broadcom claimed

insights.claim: The T-Mobile case resembles a privately settled Broadcom-AT&T dispute and an ongoing case with Tesco over VMware support.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A repeat pattern of large customers litigating signals systemic friction in Broadcom's VMware monetization that could invite regulatory or reputational risk.
insights.locator: The case is similar to a privately settled case

insights.claim: Broadcom argued thousands upon thousands of customers have already migrated to subscription and accepted end-of-availability terms, framing T-Mobile as an outlier.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Broadcom's own claim implies high subscription-conversion success across its base, supporting its revenue thesis despite the disputes.
insights.quote: Thousands upon thousands have successfully migrated to subscription. T-Mobile is the outlier here that is in litigation.
insights.locator: As noted by The Register, Broadcom previously argued

insights.claim: The financial and technical burden of suing a conglomerate like Broadcom may deter smaller organizations from litigating over VMware terms.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Litigation costs shield Broadcom from broader pushback, sustaining its pricing leverage over the long tail of customers.
